sections.title: Introduction to Indirect Object Pronouns
sections.content: Indirect object pronouns (COI - complément d'objet indirect) replace the indirect object of a verb, which is introduced by the preposition 'à' (to). They answer the question 'to whom?' or 'for whom?' after the verb.
examples: Je parle à Marie → Je lui parle (I speak to Marie → I speak to her)
Tu donnes un cadeau à Paul → Tu lui donnes un cadeau (You give a gift to Paul → You give him a gift)
Il téléphone à ses amis → Il leur téléphone (He telephones his friends → He telephones them)

sections.title: Indirect Object Pronouns
sections.content: The indirect object pronouns are: me, te, lui, nous, vous, leur. Note that lui and leur are used only for indirect objects (to him, to her, to them).
examples: lui → to/for him, to/for her: Je parle à Jean → Je lui parle (I speak to John → I speak to him)
leur → to/for them: Je donne à Marie et Paul → Je leur donne (I give to Marie and Paul → I give to them)
me → to/for me: Tu donnes à moi → Tu me donnes (You give to me → You give to me)
te → to/for you: Je parle à toi → Je te parle (I speak to you → I speak to you)

sections.title: Placement
sections.content: Indirect object pronouns come BEFORE the conjugated verb, just like direct object pronouns.
examples: Je lui parle (I speak to him/her)
Tu leur donnes le livre (You give them the book)
Il me téléphone (He telephones me)
Nous vous écrivons (We write to you)

sections.title: Y as Indirect Object Pronoun
sections.content: Y is used to replace a place or thing introduced by 'à'. It's commonly used with verbs that take 'à' as a preposition.
examples: Je vais à Paris → J'y vais (I go to Paris → I go there)
Tu penses à ton voyage → Tu y penses (You think about your trip → You think about it)
Il participe à la réunion → Il y participe (He participates in the meeting → He participates in it)
Nous croyons à cette histoire → Nous y croyons (We believe this story → We believe it)

sections.title: Verbs that Use À
sections.content: Many verbs require the preposition 'à' before their indirect object. Common examples include: parler à (to speak to), donner à (to give to), téléphoner à (to telephone), écrire à (to write to), répondre à (to answer), plaire à (to please).
examples: parler à quelqu'un (to speak to someone)
donner quelque chose à quelqu'un (to give something to someone)
téléphoner à quelqu'un (to telephone someone)
écrire à quelqu'un (to write to someone)
répondre à quelqu'un (to answer someone)

sections.title: Direct vs Indirect Object Pronouns
sections.content: Direct object pronouns (le, la, les) replace things/persons directly affected by the action. Indirect object pronouns (lui, leur) replace recipients introduced by 'à'. Some verbs can take both.
examples: Je donne le livre à Marie → Je le lui donne (I give the book to Marie → I give it to her) - both pronouns
Je vois Marie (I see Marie) → Je la vois (I see her) - direct object only
Je parle à Marie (I speak to Marie) → Je lui parle (I speak to her) - indirect object only

sections.title: Combining Pronouns
sections.content: When both direct and indirect object pronouns are used, the order is: me/te/nous/vous + le/la/les + lui/leur. In affirmative commands: lui/leur + me/te/nous/vous + le/la/les.
examples: Je le lui donne (I give it to him)
Je la leur montre (I show it to them)
Donne-le-lui! (Give it to him!)
Montre-la-leur! (Show it to them!)

sections.title: Practice Exercise
sections.content: Rewrite these sentences with indirect object pronouns: 1) Je parle à mon père. 2) Tu donnes un cadeau à ta mère. 3) Il écrit à ses grands-parents. 4) Nous répondons au professeur. 5) Vous téléphonez à vos amis.
